rule_id: # Merge into ontology/rules.csv after cardiologist validation.
# Sgarbossa criterion 1: Concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in LBBB → STEMI-equivalent
# Wellens Pattern A: Biphasic T-waves in V2-V3 → proximal LAD occlusion warning
# de Winter T-waves: ST depression + tall T-waves = STEMI-equivalent (anterior)
# Reciprocal changes: ST depression in inferior leads during anterior STEMI
# When model sees both ST_Elevation and ST_Depression simultaneously
# Posterior MI: ST depression in V1-V4 may represent posterior STEMI
# Boost NSTEMI → STEMI when high Pai on ST_Depression without clear lateral elevation
# Right ventricular MI: inferior STEMI + RV involvement (ST elevation in V4R)
# STEMI with syncope (hypotension) → likely RV involvement
# Anterior STEMI with LBBB: Sgarbossa criterion — highest specificity
# Torsades de Pointes trigger: Long QT + palpitations → VT-equivalent risk
# Clinically treat as VT until proven otherwise
# Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern: Short PR + delta wave → SVT mechanism
# AF with aberrant conduction: Wide complex AF can mimic VT
# Mutual exclusion: if AF pai > VT pai and wide QRS
# Sinus bradycardia + chest pain → inferior MI with vagal response (Bezold-Jarisch)
# High-degree AV block during inferior MI: vagal → 2nd or 3rd degree block
# Boosts heart block derivation when STEMI + dizziness
# Frequent PVCs (>10%) in setting of CAD → increased VT risk
# SVT in young patient without structural disease: likely AVNRT or AVRT
# AF with rapid ventricular response + syncope → hemodynamically unstable
# LVH + Strain: lateral ST depression in LVH patient is strain
# Reduces NSTEMI score when LVH is predominant finding
# RBBB + right axis deviation: suggests RV strain / pulmonary hypertension
# Boost RBBB score when combined with dyspnea
# LBBB: New-onset LBBB in setting of HF → possible CRT indication
# Dilated cardiomyopathy pattern: LBBB + LVH + dyspnea → heart failure
# VF always overrides: if VF detected
# Already in rules.csv as R2a but adding explicit safety escalation
# Pulseless VT = VF-equivalent clinically → escalate treatment
# Young patient (<40) with STEMI without RF → consider coronary spasm / drug use
# STEMI with no symptoms (incidental find) → likely pericarditis mimic
# NSTEMI with no risk factors + young: more likely demand ischemia (Type 2 MI)
# Sinus tachycardia as primary rhythm in setting of pain → not primary arrhythmia
# Reflects that ST is a response
